3 min read
1 hour ago

--

New releases of C are historically: not that significant. But C29 changes that. For the first time, it becomes important to not stick to the past. C99 and C11, very commonly used must now migrate. Because the promise is incredible. And the reason, simple, 1 single word. But to understand the reason it is so important, we must first understand the problem.
Memory Leaks — The Plague of C
I think that heading is good enough to tell where this is going. Memory leaks are the biggest problem in low level languages. C and C++, that’s why Rust is so popular. Both C and C++ have tools like valgrind to help you find them, but that’s just not enough. C++ also has RAII wrappers for everything, but they add that small overhead and lots of old code doesn’t use them. And well, legacy code doesn’t use them as much. Checkout my previous article on C++29 for more information on how C++ is solving the problem.
Memory leaks are so massive that literally every application in the world has them. No matter how big or how small they are. (There are a few exceptions of course like incredibly well maintained open-source tools). And most of those come from, C, and that is what is being fixed in C29.
The Defer Keyword
I told you there was one word that would solve the problem and this is it, defer. Historically, exception handling in C involves adding if statements for everything that returns an error code. But what if you allocated memory before this? Now every if statement needs to call free on every single chunk of memory allocated previously. And there is no solution, you just have to do it. This is also a big problem for large code where there’s dozens of error throwing function calls in each function. SO MUCH TO FREE.
A simple solution is to use goto . For dozens of very simple reasons, this is a horrible approach, but sometimes, what can you do? It saves so you much typing, but now you have to write a custom goto for every single section that has different memory and remember to call it. The problem is never solved. So now let’s finally talk about the defer keyword. The defer keyword is similar to that of a language like Go. You tell the compiler that this must be executed before this function exits, no matter how that happens. Now, you can say defer free(my_allocated_memory); and that’s it. Now there is no need for every if statement to have the memory free call and it reduces memory leaks drastically. The compiler now has control over the generated function and it will put that free statement everywhere.
Obviously, there are more usages for defer than just free but those are less commonly used because what else do you need to ensure happens at every ending path of a function? But, there will be definitely be some use cases for this.
The Compiler Has Control
Historically, the C paradigm has been: “give the developer full control”. You knew exactly what the binary was going to look like when you wrote that code. Simple, plain and clear, the compiler’s job was just do the conversion. But now, this is a change. The compiler changes how your function looks when compiled. It has power. What was historically a C++ paradigm has now done the smallest change into C. You still know exactly what the binary looks like, it’s just not right there when you look at the code section.
